metadata.title: Wrap It in a UI
metadata.playlist: Wrap It & Test It — INFO 7375 Assignment 5A
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5A brief — Interface, User Testing, and Market Positioning (Path A)

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Your Madison tool works — in code, for you. Assignment 5A wants a stranger to open a URL and use it without your help. That's the only job an interface has: put a box around your tool that someone who isn't you can drive. Let me show you how to build one.

props.command: My Madison tool works in a script. How do I wrap it in an interface a stranger can use?
output: Pick Gradio or Streamlit — 20-second decision, don't shop.
Wire input fields from Part 3 data contract with plain-English labels.
Output something human-readable. Never JSON. Never a traceback.

beats.narration_text: Two tools, one decision, twenty seconds. Gradio is fastest for tools that take input and return output — a text box in, formatted answer out. It's what Hugging Face Spaces runs natively, which matters for your deploy step. Streamlit gives more layout control: columns, tabs, richer pages. If your tool is an input-to-output pipeline — and most Madison tools are — start with Gradio. Don't shop. Pick one and build. You can always migrate; right now you need something deployed.

shot.new_visual_element: Gradio vs Streamlit decision matrix — two options, one 20-second rule

beats.act: wiring input fields from the data contract
beats.narration_text: Open your Part 3 data contract. Every field your tool takes as input becomes a labeled widget in the interface. A text string becomes a Textbox — label it what the user is actually entering, not your Python variable name. 'user_query' is not a label. 'What would you like to know?' is a label. A choice between options becomes a Dropdown with those options listed. A number becomes a Slider or Number input with a sensible range and a unit. Read each label out loud as if you are the Part 2 user reading it for the first time, with no context about what the tool does. If the instruction is not obvious from the label alone — rewrite it.

shot.new_visual_element: Input field types mapped from Part 3 data contract: Textbox / Dropdown / Slider with label examples

beats.narration_text: Your tool outputs something. The question is what form. The rubric names the trap directly: a raw JSON blob is wrong. A dataframe with column headers is wrong. The output must be something your Part 2 user can read and act on — without asking you what it means. That means Markdown-formatted text, a clean human-readable summary, a chart, or a downloadable file. One test: show the output to someone who does not know what the tool does. Can they tell, in ten seconds, what it means and what to do next? If they're reading keys and curly braces, that's your revision target.

shot.new_visual_element: Output format comparison: JSON blob (wrong) vs formatted text/chart/file (right)

props.sparkLine: Can they act on it in ten seconds?

beats.narration_text: Two things will go wrong when someone who isn't you uses it. The input will be empty. And the tool will fail on something unexpected. Both need a plain-English message — not a Python traceback, not 'Internal server error.' Wrap your tool call in a try-except. If input is empty, return: 'Please enter a question to get started.' If the tool fails, return something human: 'Something went wrong — try a shorter question.' The point is not to catch every bug. The point is that a confused user gets one clear sentence instead of a wall of red text.

shot.new_visual_element: Code pattern: empty-input guard + try/except with plain-English messages

props.title: Error handling — the two cases
props.sparkLine: Name the problem in plain English.

beats.narration_text: Four things that make the interface pass. Labeled inputs your Part 2 user can understand without your help. A human-readable output — not JSON, not a dataframe. Plain-English error messages — not a traceback. And the test question that ties them together: could my Part 2 user finish a task without me in the room? That one question is the whole rubric.

artifactLines: Labeled inputs: widget type matches field type; label is an instruction, not a variable name.
Human-readable output: formatted text, chart, or file — never a JSON blob or raw dataframe.
Plain-English errors: empty-input message + tool-failure message, no Python tracebacks.
The test: could your Part 2 user finish a task without you in the room? Build until yes.

beats.narration_text: Your Turn. Here is the prompt to scaffold your interface. Paste your Part 3 data contract fields, your tool's output description, and your Part 2 user description into it. Claude will draft a Gradio app with labeled inputs, a formatted output section, and the two error guards. Read the labels carefully — that's where your judgment matters, not Claude's.

props.command: Build a Gradio interface for my Madison tool. Tool inputs from Part 3: [paste fields]. Output: [describe what the tool returns]. Part 2 user: [describe them]. Requirements: plain-English labels (not variable names), formatted human-readable output (no JSON), empty-input message, and a try/except failure message. Show me the full app.py.
props.runningText: paste this into Claude with your Part 3 fields filled in…

beats.narration_text: Wrap It in a UI. Liam, in for Bear.
